steps.title: De Boterham
steps.desc: Welcome to Unit 4: Eten & Drinken. We start with the most Dutch thing imaginable — lunch. In the Netherlands, lunch is cold bread. Every day. With cheese. Or hagelslag (chocolate sprinkles). This lesson teaches essential food nouns, articles, and the cultural shock that is Dutch lunch.
goals: Say what you eat and drink

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Dutch Lunch: The Culture Shock
steps.text:
In France, lunch is a warm three-course affair.
In Spain, lunch is the main meal of the day.
In the Netherlands, lunch is... bread.

Cold bread. With cheese. Or peanut butter. Or hagelslag.
Every single day. At your desk. From a broodtrommel.

The Dutch call this 'de boterham' — a slice of bread with something on it.
That's it. That's lunch.

Don't fight it. Embrace the boterham. It's efficient, practical, and very Dutch.

deepDive.title: Why bread? A theory.
deepDive.text:
The Netherlands is a trading nation. Time is money.
A warm lunch takes 45–60 minutes. A boterham takes 10.

The Dutch word 'boterham' literally comes from:
boter (butter) + ham (ham) — bread with butter.

The broodtrommel (bread box/lunch box) is a Dutch icon.
Children bring them to school. Adults bring them to work.

The WARM meal is dinner ('het avondeten'), served at 18:00 sharp.
Being late for dinner is a greater sin than being late for work.

steps.also: also a noun: 'het eten' = the food/the meal
steps.note:
Irregular verb! ik eet, jij eet, hij eet.
The stam is 'eet' (long vowel), not 'et'.
Plural: wij eten (same as infinitive).

Long 'ee' — like English 'ate' without the t.
Double 'e' in closed syllable = long vowel.

Wait — where's the -t? Stam = 'eet'.
eet + t would be 'eett' — Dutch never doubles a final -t.
So: hij eet. No extra -t needed.

steps.title: Eten: to eat
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
Stam = eet. Since 'eet' already ends in -t, no extra -t is added for jij/hij.
All singular forms are identical: eet.

Regular verb! Stam = drink.
ik drink, jij drinkt, hij drinkt.
Almost identical to English.

Stam + t = drinkt. Regular rule.
Compare 'eten': stam already ends in -t → no extra.
'Drinken': stam = drink → add -t → drinkt.

Het-word. The single most important food in Dutch culture.
Plural: broden.

steps.example: Ik eet een boterham met kaas.
steps.exampleEn: I eat a sandwich with cheese.
steps.note:
De-word. Literally: butter-ham.
A slice of bread with a topping = een boterham.
Plural: boterhammen.

steps.exampleEn: The Netherlands makes a lot of cheese.
steps.note:
De-word. From Latin 'caseus' — borrowed into Germanic
languages 2000 years ago via Roman trade routes.
The Netherlands is the world's largest cheese exporter.
Gouda, Edam, Leiden — all Dutch cities AND cheeses.

De-word. Almost identical to English.
Karnemelk (buttermilk) is uniquely popular in NL.

Het-word. Short word, big breakfast item.
Plural: eieren (irregular — adds -eren).

De-word. You can hear it in 'boterham'!
Almost identical across all Germanic languages.

steps.title: 🧀 What Goes on a Boterham?
steps.text:
The classic Dutch lunch toppings:

kaas = cheese (the #1 choice)
boter = butter (the base layer)
pindakaas = peanut butter ('peanut cheese'!)
hagelslag = chocolate sprinkles (yes, on bread)
ham = ham
jam = jam

The combo: boter + kaas = 'een boterham met kaas.'
The kids' favourite: boter + hagelslag.

'Met' = with. You'll use this word constantly:
brood met kaas = bread with cheese
koffie met melk = coffee with milk
thee met suiker = tea with sugar

steps.note:
Identical to German 'mit'.
You'll use this in almost every food sentence:
'een boterham met...' = a sandwich with...

De-word. The Dutch drink MORE coffee per person
than almost any country. 3-4 cups a day is normal.
The 'koffiepauze' (coffee break) is sacred.

De-word. Same word across many languages.
'Thee' has a long 'ee' sound — like 'tay'.

steps.title: ☕ Dutch Coffee Culture
steps.text:
The Dutch are among the world's top coffee drinkers.

Normal coffee = 'een bakkie koffie' (informal)
With milk = 'koffie met melk'
Black = 'zwarte koffie'

The 'koffiepauze' (coffee break) is a workplace ritual.
Refusing coffee when offered is... unusual.

'Wil je koffie?' = Do you want coffee?
This is the most common sentence in Dutch offices.

You'll learn 'willen' (to want) in the next lesson!

opts: the main meal of the day

steps.s: Ik eet brood {1} kaas. Zij drinkt koffie {2} melk.
steps.hint: bread WITH cheese, coffee WITH milk

steps.q: Which of these is a het-word?
steps.hint: Think about what you just learned — which one had 'het'?

steps.title: Ik wil graag...
steps.desc: Your first modal verb: willen (to want). This is the single most useful sentence pattern for surviving in the Netherlands. 'Ik wil graag een kopje koffie' will get you through any café, restaurant, or office kitchen.

steps.note:
Your first MODAL verb.
Modals are special: they change the word order.
The second verb goes to the END of the sentence.

No -t, no tricks. Just 'wil'.
Short 'i' — like English 'will'.

Technically: stam + t = wilt.
In questions: 'Wil jij...?' (-t drops as always).
But in practice: most Dutch speakers say 'jij wil'
EVEN in statements. 'Jij wil koffie' is more natural
than 'Jij wilt koffie' in everyday speech.
Written/formal: jij wilt. Spoken: jij wil. Both correct.

Wait — no -t? Correct!
'Willen' is irregular: hij wil (not 'wilt').
This is one of the few verbs where hij has no -t.

steps.title: Willen: to want
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
Irregular! hij/zij wil (no -t). jij wilt OR jij wil — both correct.
In daily speech, 'jij wil' dominates. 'Jij wilt' sounds more formal.
Plural = willen. Two forms to know: wil and willen.

steps.title: 📐 Modal Verb Word Order
steps.text:
Modal verbs change the sentence structure.
The second verb moves to the END:

Without modal:
Ik drink koffie. = I drink coffee.

With modal:
Ik wil koffie drinken. = I want to drink coffee.

The pattern: subject + modal + object + INFINITIVE (at the end)

Ik wil brood eten. = I want to eat bread.
Hij wil water drinken. = He wants to drink water.
Wij willen kaas eten. = We want to eat cheese.

⚠️ The infinitive goes to the VERY end.
This feels strange if you speak English,
but natural if you know German (same rule!).

steps.example: Ik wil graag een kopje koffie.
steps.exampleEn: I would like a cup of coffee.
steps.note:
'Graag' is the magic politeness word.
'Ik wil koffie' = I want coffee. (blunt)
'Ik wil graag koffie' = I'd like coffee. (polite)
Always add 'graag' when ordering!

Het-word. Derives from 'kop' which means HEAD.
'Kopje' (kop + je) literally = 'small head'.
Three meanings in daily Dutch:
1. A small cup (een kopje koffie)
2. A small head (affectionate: 'een lief kopje')
3. A heading/header in text (een kopje in een artikel)
The '-je' is a diminutive — you'll learn this system in L6.
Same root as English 'cup' — both from Latin 'cuppa'.

Het-word. Identical to English and German.
Plural: glazen (the 's' becomes 'z' — devoicing in reverse).

steps.example: Ik wil graag een glas water.
steps.exampleEn: I would like a glass of water.
steps.note:
Het-word. Almost identical to English.
Remember: Dutch 'w' sounds like English 'v'.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Ordering in Dutch: The Formula
steps.text:
The polite ordering formula:

Ik wil graag + [quantity] + [item].

Ik wil graag een kopje koffie. = I'd like a cup of coffee.
Ik wil graag een glas water. = I'd like a glass of water.
Ik wil graag een boterham met kaas. = I'd like a cheese sandwich.

Even shorter (very common):
Een kopje koffie, alsjeblieft. = A cup of coffee, please.
Een glas water, alsjeblieft. = A glass of water, please.

Both work. The short version is perfectly polite.
The Dutch value directness — no need for elaborate formulas.

deepDive.title: Terrasje: the Dutch outdoor café
deepDive.text:
When the sun comes out in the Netherlands, the entire country moves outside.

'Op het terras zitten' = to sit on the terrace.
'Een terrasje pakken' = to grab a spot on a terrace.

This is a national ritual. The first sunny day of spring,
every café terrace in Amsterdam is packed by noon.

'Gezellig' — that untranslatable Dutch word for cosy,
warm, social, pleasant — this is what a terrasje is.

You'll learn 'gezellig' properly later. For now:
terrasje = outdoor café = Dutch happiness.

steps.note:
Very common small word.
'Ik ook!' = Me too!
'Zij ook.' = Her too.
English once had the same word: 'eke' meant 'also.'
You can still see it hiding in 'nickname' — originally
'an eke-name' (an also-name, an extra name).
The word shifted over centuries. Dutch kept 'ook', English lost 'eke.'

Same as English 'of'? No — it means 'or'.
False friend alert!
Dutch 'of' = or. English 'of' = van.

steps.q: 'Ik wil graag een kopje koffie.' means:
opts: I need a cup of coffee.
I would like a cup of coffee.
I have a cup of coffee.
I drink a cup of coffee.
steps.ans: I would like a cup of coffee.

steps.hint: Modal: infinitive goes to the END

opts: Willen is irregular: hij wil, no -t
steps.ans: Willen is irregular: hij wil, no -t
steps.hint: One of the few verbs where hij has no -t

steps.s: Ik wil graag een {1} koffie. Koffie {2} thee?
steps.hint: A cup of coffee. Coffee or tea?

steps.q: Where does the infinitive go with a modal verb?
opts: At the end of the sentence
steps.ans: At the end of the sentence

steps.title: Kunnen & Mogen
steps.desc: English technically has both 'can' (ability) and 'may' (permission) — but modern speakers almost always say 'can' for both. 'Can I sit here?' has largely replaced 'May I sit here?' Dutch doesn't allow this collapse. Kunnen stays kunnen (ability). Mogen stays mogen (permission). The distinction is alive and enforced.

steps.note:
Modal verb #2. Ability = kunnen.
'Can you swim?' = 'Kun je zwemmen?'
Same word order rule: infinitive at the END.
In English, 'can' has swallowed 'may' in everyday speech.
In Dutch, kunnen ONLY covers ability — never permission.
'Can I sit here?' in Dutch must be 'Mag ik...?' (mogen), never 'Kan ik...?'

Short 'a' — like 'can' in English.
No -t, no tricks. Just 'kan'.

kunt OR kan — both are correct for jij.
In questions: 'Kun jij...?' (-t drops).
'Kan jij...?' is also fine and very common.

Irregular again! hij kan (no -t).
Same as willen: modals often skip the -t for hij.

steps.title: Kunnen: can / to be able to
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
Irregular: hij/zij kan (no -t). jij kunt OR jij kan — both correct.
Same pattern as willen: two forms (kan / kunnen).

Modal verb #3. Permission = mogen.
This is the POLITE request verb.
'Mag ik...?' = May I...? (very common)
English DOES have 'may' for permission — 'May I sit here?'
But most English speakers now say 'Can I sit here?' instead.
In Dutch you cannot make that swap. 'Kan ik hier zitten?'
would sound like you're asking about physical ability,
not permission. Mogen stays mogen. Always.

steps.en: I may / I am allowed to
steps.exampleEn: I am not allowed to smoke here.
steps.note:
Short 'a'. The 'g' is the Dutch throat sound.
'Mag niet' = not allowed. You'll hear this constantly.

steps.en: he may / he is allowed to
steps.note:
Irregular: hij mag (no -t). All three modals so far:
hij wil, hij kan, hij mag. No -t on any of them.

steps.title: Mogen: may / to be allowed to
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
Irregular: ALL singular forms are 'mag'. No -t anywhere.
jij mag (not 'magt'). Same pattern as willen and kunnen.

steps.title: 🔑 Kunnen vs Mogen: The Split
steps.text:
English uses 'can' for both. Dutch splits them:

KUNNEN = ability (physical/mental)
Ik kan zwemmen. = I can swim. (I have the ability.)
Kun je fietsen? = Can you cycle? (Do you know how?)
Hij kan goed koken. = He can cook well.

MOGEN = permission (allowed/not allowed)
Mag ik hier zitten? = May I sit here?
Je mag niet roken. = You may not smoke.
Mogen wij naar binnen? = May we go inside?

⚠️ The test: replace 'can' with 'am allowed to'.
If it works → mogen. If not → kunnen.

'Can I sit here?' → 'Am I allowed to sit here?' ✅ → MOGEN
'Can you swim?' → 'Are you allowed to swim?' ❌ → KUNNEN

deepDive.title: German speakers: careful!
deepDive.text:
German 'mögen' means 'to like' (ich mag Kaffee = I like coffee).
Dutch 'mogen' means 'to be allowed to'.

Dutch 'may I?' = Mag ik?
German 'may I?' = Darf ich?

Dutch 'I like coffee' = Ik hou van koffie. (NOT 'ik mag koffie')
German 'I like coffee' = Ich mag Kaffee.

False friend across the border!

steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = betaal.
Very useful: 'Kan ik betalen?' = Can I pay?
This is how you ask for the bill.

Stam = zit. Cognate of English 'sit'.
'Mag ik hier zitten?' — you'll use this on every terras.

Regular verb. Stam = rook.
Smoking is banned indoors in NL since 2008.
'Niet roken' signs are everywhere.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 'Dat mag niet!'
steps.text:
Three words you'll hear constantly in the Netherlands:

'Dat mag niet!' = That's not allowed!

The Dutch are direct about rules. If something is forbidden,
they'll tell you — clearly, immediately, without softening.

Dat mag niet. = That's not allowed.
Je mag hier niet roken. = You may not smoke here.
Je mag hier niet fietsen. = You may not cycle here.
Dat mag niet van de gemeente. = The municipality doesn't allow that.

This directness isn't rude — it's Dutch efficiency.
You know exactly where you stand.

steps.q: 'Mag ik hier zitten?' is asking for:

steps.hint: Permission to sit. Ability to cycle.

steps.q: All three modals (wil, kan, mag) share which quirk?
opts: They all end in -t for hij
They all skip the -t for hij
steps.ans: They all skip the -t for hij
steps.hint: hij wil, hij kan, hij mag — no -t

opts: Can I pay here? (is it possible?)
steps.ans: Can I pay here? (is it possible?)

steps.title: Moeten & Hoeven
steps.desc: Modal #4: moeten (must/have to). Plus its negative partner: hoeven (need to). English has no single verb for 'don't have to' — Dutch does. This lesson also teaches Dutch punctuality culture, because 'je moet op tijd komen' is a way of life here.
goals: Use hoeven + niet for 'don't have to'

steps.note:
Modal verb #4. Obligation = moeten.
Same word order: infinitive at the end.
Ik moet werken = I must work.

steps.example: Ik moet om negen uur beginnen.
steps.exampleEn: I have to start at nine o'clock.
steps.note:
Long 'oe' sound (like 'oo' in 'food').
Strong obligation — 'I have no choice'.

steps.exampleEn: You have to come on time!
steps.note:
Same form as ik: 'moet'. No extra -t.
Stam = moet, which already ends in -t.
Same logic as eten: eet + t would be 'eett'.

steps.exampleEn: He has to work every day.
steps.note:
Again: moet. The stam ends in -t, so no extra -t.
All singular forms of moeten are identical: moet.

steps.title: Moeten: must / have to
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
All singular = moet. Why no extra -t for jij/hij?
Stam = moet (ends in -t already). Same rule as eten.
Two forms: moet (singular) and moeten (plural).

The negative partner of moeten.
ALWAYS used with 'niet': 'hoeft niet' = don't have to.
English needs 3 words. Dutch has one verb.

steps.en: you don't have to / you needn't
steps.note:
hoeft + niet + te + infinitive.
Note the 'te' before the infinitive — unique to hoeven.
Modals normally don't need 'te'. Hoeven does.
This little word 'te' will come back later in Unit 9
with 'om...te' (in order to). For now, just memorize it
as part of the hoeven formula: hoeft + niet + te + verb.

steps.title: ⚖️ Moeten vs Hoeven
steps.text:
MOETEN = must / have to (positive obligation)
Je moet op tijd komen. = You must come on time.
Ik moet werken. = I have to work.

HOEVEN + NIET = don't have to / needn't (no obligation)
Je hoeft niet te komen. = You don't have to come.
Je hoeft niet te betalen. = You don't need to pay.

⚠️ Key difference:
'Je moet niet...' = You must NOT... (prohibition)
'Je hoeft niet...' = You don't HAVE TO... (no obligation)

'Je moet niet roken.' = You must not smoke. (forbidden)
'Je hoeft niet te roken.' = You don't have to smoke. (your choice)

Big difference! 'Moet niet' is much stronger than 'hoeft niet'.

deepDive.title: Why 'te' with hoeven?
deepDive.text:
All modal verbs so far use bare infinitives:
Ik wil eten. (not 'te eten')
Ik kan zwemmen. (not 'te zwemmen')
Ik moet werken. (not 'te werken')

But hoeven is special:
Ik hoef niet TE betalen.

Why? Hoeven is technically not a 'true' modal —
it's a semi-modal that requires 'te' before the infinitive.

Just remember: hoeven + niet + te + infinitive.
Je hoeft niet te komen.
Wij hoeven niet te werken.

steps.example: Ik moet om negen uur beginnen.
steps.exampleEn: I have to start at nine o'clock.
steps.note:
Identical to English! Regular verb.
Stam = begin. Ik begin, jij begint.

Op = on, tijd = time.
The Dutch live by this phrase.
'Op tijd' is not a suggestion — it's a lifestyle.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Je Moet Op Tijd Komen!
steps.text:
The Dutch and time:

Meeting at 14:00? Be there at 13:55.
Dinner at 18:00? Don't arrive at 18:15.
Train at 08:12? It leaves at 08:12.

Remember 'op tijd' and 'te laat' from Lesson 7?
Now combine them with 'moeten':

'Op tijd' (on time) is a core Dutch value.
'Te laat' (late) is a genuine social offence.

At work: 'Je moet op tijd beginnen.' = You must start on time.
At school: 'Je moet op tijd komen.' = You must come on time.
For dinner: 'We eten om zes uur.' = We eat at six. (Be there.)

The Dutch saying: 'Afspraak is afspraak.'
= A deal is a deal. / An appointment is an appointment.
If you said 14:00, you meant 14:00.

steps.title: 📋 All Four Modals — Compared
steps.text:
willen = to want → Ik wil koffie drinken.
kunnen = can (ability) → Ik kan Nederlands spreken.
mogen = may (permission) → Mag ik hier zitten?
moeten = must (obligation) → Ik moet werken.

All four share the same word order:
Subject + modal + object + INFINITIVE (at end).

All four are irregular in the singular:
hij wil, hij kan, hij mag, hij moet.
(willen/kunnen/mogen skip -t; moeten's stam already ends in -t)

Negative obligation = hoeven + niet + te + infinitive:
Je hoeft niet te betalen.

deepDive.title: The modal family tree
deepDive.text:
All four Dutch modals are ancient Germanic words with English cousins:

willen ↔ will — English 'will' started as 'to want' (I will = I want to).
Over time it became a future marker. Dutch kept the original 'want' meaning.

kunnen ↔ can — Nearly identical. Both from Proto-Germanic *kunnaną.
In Old English, 'can' also meant 'to know how.' Dutch 'kennen' (to know)
is the same root.

mogen ↔ may — Both from *maganą (to be able/allowed).
English 'may' is dying in casual speech — most people say 'can' instead.
Dutch 'mogen' is alive and used daily.

moeten ↔ must — Both from *mōtaną.
English 'must' has no past tense ('I musted' doesn't exist).
Dutch 'moeten' does: ik moest (I had to). Much more flexible.

Notice the pattern: Dutch modals are more regular and more alive
than their English cousins. English is simplifying; Dutch is holding firm.

steps.q: What's the difference between 'moet niet' and 'hoeft niet'?
opts: 'moet niet' = must not, 'hoeft niet' = don't have to
'moet niet' = don't have to, 'hoeft niet' = must not
steps.ans: 'moet niet' = must not, 'hoeft niet' = don't have to

steps.s: Je {1} op tijd komen. Je {2} niet te betalen.
steps.hint: Must come on time. Don't have to pay.

steps.s: Wij ___ niet te werken op zondag.
steps.hint: We don't HAVE TO work on Sunday

steps.q: Which modal verb needs 'te' before the infinitive?
'Afspraak is afspraak' reflects the Dutch value of:

steps.s: Hij ___ elke dag om zeven uur beginnen.
steps.hint: He HAS TO start every day at seven

steps.title: Mijn Koffie, Jouw Broodje
steps.desc: You've been ordering food but you can't say 'my coffee' or 'your sandwich' yet. Time to fix that. Dutch possessives are straightforward — and surprisingly close to English.
goals: 5 possessive pronouns (mijn, jouw, zijn, haar, uw)
Use possessives with food and drink

steps.note:
Sounds like English 'mine'.
Unstressed form: 'm'n' (very common in speech).
Written: mijn. Spoken: often m'n.

From 'jij' → jouw (possessive).
Unstressed form: 'je' (same as the pronoun!).
Context makes it clear: 'je koffie' = your coffee.

Yes — same word as 'zijn' (to be)!
'Hij is groot.' = He is big. (verb)
'Zijn huis is groot.' = His house is big. (possessive)
Context always makes it clear.

Same word as 'haar' (hair).
Another case of Dutch polysemy.
'Haar haar is lang.' = Her hair is long. (really!)

steps.title: 📋 All Possessives
steps.text:
mijn = my (unstressed: m'n)
jouw = your informal (unstressed: je)
zijn = his / its (unstressed: z'n)
haar = her
uw = your formal
ons / onze = our
jullie = your (plural)
hun = their

For now, focus on the first four: mijn, jouw, zijn, haar.
You'll use these 95% of the time.
Ons/jullie/hun come in Unit 5.

deepDive.title: Stressed vs unstressed
deepDive.text:
In casual Dutch speech, possessives often shorten:

mijn → m'n (mun)
jouw → je (yuh)
zijn → z'n (zun)

Written Dutch: Mijn koffie is koud.
Spoken Dutch: M'n koffie is koud.

Both are correct. In formal writing, use the full form.
In speech, the short forms are more natural.

You'll hear 'je' a lot — it can mean:
1. you (pronoun): 'Je bent groot.' = You are big.
2. your (possessive): 'Je koffie is koud.' = Your coffee is cold.
Context always tells you which one.

steps.note:
Formal 'your'. Used with 'u'.
Hear it in restaurants and shops:
'Uw bestelling?' = Your order?

De-word. You'll need this in every restaurant.
'De rekening, alsjeblieft.' = The bill, please.
Cognate of English 'reckoning' (counting up).

steps.title: ☕ Whose Order Is This?
steps.text:
At a café, the server brings drinks:

Uw koffie, mevrouw. = Your coffee, madam.
Uw thee, meneer. = Your tea, sir.

At your table with friends:
Dit is mijn koffie. = This is my coffee.
Dat is jouw broodje. = That is your sandwich.
Zijn glas is leeg. = His glass is empty.
Haar thee is koud. = Her tea is cold.

Asking for the bill:
Mag ik de rekening? = May I have the bill?
De rekening, alsjeblieft. = The bill, please.

🇳🇱 Dutch custom: splitting the bill ('going Dutch')
is genuinely common in the Netherlands!

steps.s: Dit is {1} koffie. Dat is {2} broodje.
steps.hint: This is MY coffee. That is YOUR sandwich.

steps.q: In a restaurant, the formal 'your' is:

steps.s: {1} glas is leeg. {2} koffie is koud.
steps.hint: HIS glass is empty. MY coffee is cold.

steps.title: Twee Broodjes, Drie Biertjes
steps.desc: Two systems that food makes real: plurals (-en and -s) and diminutives (-je and friends). In Dutch, 'een biertje' isn't just a small beer — it's a FRIENDLY beer. Diminutives change the social temperature of a word.

steps.title: 📦 Two Ways to Make Plurals
steps.text:
Remember the open/closed syllable rules from Foundations?
They're about to matter again. Spelling changes kick in with -en plurals.

Dutch has two plural endings:

-EN (the default — most nouns)
brood → broden = breads
boterham → boterhammen = sandwiches
ei → eieren = eggs (irregular)

-S (for words ending in -el, -em, -en, -er, -je)
tafel → tafels = tables
appel → appels = apples
broodje → broodjes = little breads

Rule of thumb:
If it ends in an unstressed syllable (-el, -em, -en, -er): add -s.
Otherwise: add -en.

⚠️ Spelling changes with -en: one vowel may drop (brood → broden)
or a consonant may double (boterham → boterhammen) to keep the
vowel sound correct. Same rules you learned in the Foundations Playthrough.

steps.note:
brood → broden. The 'oo' becomes 'o'.
Why? 'bro-den' = open syllable → long vowel.
One 'o' is enough. (Foundations: vowel dropping!)

boterham → boterhammen.
The 'm' doubles! Why?
'ham' + en → 'ha-men' would make 'a' long.
Double 'm' keeps the 'a' short.

steps.title: ⚙️ Spelling Changes in Plurals
steps.text:
When adding -en, the Foundations spelling rules kick in:

VOWEL DROPPING (long vowel, open syllable → drop one letter):
brood → bro-den (not 'brooden') — one 'o' is enough
kaas → ka-zen (not 'kaazen') — one 'a' is enough

CONSONANT DOUBLING (short vowel needs protection):
boterham → boterhám-men (double 'm' keeps 'a' short)
glas → glá-zen (irregular: s → z, see below)

VOICING (s → z, f → v in plurals):
glas → glazen (s → z between vowels)
huis → huizen (s → z)

These are the SAME rules from Foundations Playthrough.
If you did the Playthrough, you already know this system!

steps.note:
De-word. Plural: appels (-s, because it ends in -el).
Identical cognate across all Germanic languages.

Simple -s plural. Words ending in -el, -em, -en, -er
get -s instead of -en. Easy.

steps.title: 🍰 Enter the Diminutive: -je
steps.text:
The diminutive is the SOUL of Dutch.

Add '-je' to make something small:
brood → broodje = small bread / roll
bier → biertje = small beer
kop → kopje = small cup

But here's the secret: diminutives aren't just about SIZE.
They change the FEELING:

'Een bier' = a beer. (neutral, factual)
'Een biertje' = a beer. (friendly, casual, cosy)

'Een moment' = a moment. (formal)
'Een momentje' = a moment. (friendly, 'just a sec')

The Dutch use diminutives CONSTANTLY.
They make everything warmer, softer, more gezellig.

⚠️ ALL diminutives are het-words!
de kop → het kopje
de hond → het hondje
de kat → het katje

steps.note:
brood + je = broodje.
Het-word (all diminutives are het!).
'Een broodje kaas' = the classic Dutch lunch order.

bier + tje = biertje.
Why -tje and not just -je? After a long vowel + r,
you need -tje. More rules below.

taart + je = taartje.
Dutch cafés always offer 'iets erbij?' (something with it?)
meaning: a cake or pastry with your coffee.

steps.title: 📐 Diminutive Spelling Rules
steps.text:
The base rule: add -je.
But Dutch pronunciation demands adjustments:

-JE: after most consonants
brood → broodje, huis → huisje, boek → boekje

-TJE: after long vowel/diphthong + l, n, r
bier → biertje, stoel → stoeltje, deur → deurtje

-PJE: after long vowel/diphthong + m
boom → boompje (tree → little tree)

-ETJE: after words ending in unstressed -el, -em, -en, -er
appel → appeltje, tafel → tafeltje

-KJE: after -ing
koning → koninkje (king → little king)

Don't memorize all rules now. The pattern:
the suffix adapts so it SOUNDS natural.
You'll develop an ear for it.

steps.q: 'Een biertje' vs 'een bier' — what's the real difference?
opts: Biertje sounds friendlier and more casual
steps.ans: Biertje sounds friendlier and more casual
steps.hint: The diminutive changes the social feeling

steps.q: Why is the plural of 'brood' written 'broden' (not 'brooden')?
opts: Open syllable: one 'o' = long vowel already
steps.ans: Open syllable: one 'o' = long vowel already

steps.q: Words ending in -el, -em, -en, -er get which plural?

steps.title: Op de Markt
steps.desc: You know numbers from Unit 3. Now use them to BUY things. This lesson teaches euro amounts, how to ask prices, and shopping vocabulary — all set at a Dutch street market.

steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = kost.
'Hoeveel kost...?' = How much does... cost?
The #1 shopping question.

steps.exampleEn: How much does a bread roll cost?
steps.note:
Hoe + veel = how + much.
You already know both words!
'Hoeveel?' works for price AND quantity.

De-word. No plural change!
'Één euro, twee euro, drie euro.'
Dutch does NOT say 'euros' — just 'euro'.

De-word. Same as English.
Plural: no change — 'vijftig cent', not 'centen'.
Prices: 'drie euro vijftig' = €3.50.

steps.title: 💰 Saying Prices in Dutch
steps.text:
Dutch price format: [euros] euro [cents]

€1.00 = één euro
€2.50 = twee euro vijftig (= twee vijftig)
€3.75 = drie euro vijfenzeventig
€0.50 = vijftig cent
€10.99 = tien euro negenennegentig

Short form (very common):
€2.50 = twee vijftig
€3.75 = drie vijfenzeventig

You can drop 'euro' in casual speech.
Just say the numbers: 'Dat is twee vijftig.'

steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = koop.
kopen + modal: 'Ik wil brood kopen.'
German cognate: kaufen.

ver- + kopen = sell.
'ver-' reverses the meaning here.
kopen = buy, verkopen = sell.

De-word. Plural: winkels.
'Winkelen' = to shop (verb form).

steps.example: Op zaterdag ga ik naar de markt.
steps.exampleEn: On Saturday I go to the market.
steps.note:
De-word. Identical cognate.
Every Dutch city has a weekly markt.
Amsterdam: Albert Cuyp. Rotterdam: Blaak.

steps.example: Kaas is goedkoop op de markt.
steps.exampleEn: Cheese is cheap at the market.
steps.note:
goed (good) + koop (buy) = 'good buy'.
Literally: a good purchase!
Opposite: duur (expensive).

Cognate of English 'dear' (as in 'costly').
The Dutch complain about prices constantly.
'Dat is te duur!' = That's too expensive!

steps.title: 🇳🇱 De Markt: Dutch Market Culture
steps.text:
Every Dutch town has a weekly market.

Amsterdam: Albert Cuypmarkt (every day!)
Rotterdam: Markthal + Blaak
Utrecht: Bloemenmarkt

What you find: verse groenten (fresh vegetables),
kaas, vis (fish), bloemen (flowers), kleding (clothing).

At the market, you'll hear:
'Zegt u het maar!' = Go ahead, tell me! (= What would you like?)
'Drie euro het kilo.' = Three euros per kilo.
'Anders nog iets?' = Anything else?

The Dutch reputation for being careful with money?
The market is where you see it in action.
'Dat is te duur' is not rude — it's negotiation.

steps.hint: How much does a roll cost? €2.50

steps.title: Borrel! — Unit 4 Review
steps.desc: It's Friday afternoon. Time for the vrijdagmiddagborrel — the most Dutch social ritual there is. This review pulls together everything from Unit 4: ordering, modals, possessives, plurals, diminutives, and prices. All set at a borrel.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 De Borrel: Dutch Social Glue
steps.text:
A borrel is an informal drinks gathering.

The vrijmibo (vrijdagmiddagborrel) = Friday afternoon drinks.
This is where Dutch colleagues become friends.

What's at a borrel:
• biertjes (beers — always diminutive, always casual)
• wijn (wine)
• bitterballen (deep-fried meat ragout balls — THE borrel snack)
• kaasblokjes (little cheese cubes)
• nootjes (nuts — another diminutive!)

The word 'borrel' itself means 'a drink' (informal).
'Een borrel doen' = to have drinks.
'Borrelen' = to socialise over drinks.

Proost! = Cheers! (always look people in the eye)

steps.q: 'Ik wil graag een biertje.' — this sentence uses:
'Hoeveel kosten drie bitterballen?' — 'kosten' is plural because:

steps.s: {1} koffie is koud. {2} biertje is lekker!
steps.hint: MY coffee is cold. YOUR beer is tasty!

steps.s: Wij ___ niet te betalen — het is gratis!
steps.hint: We don't HAVE TO pay — it's free!

steps.q: 'Twee broodjes kaas en drie biertjes.' — how many items total?

steps.s: Hij {1} altijd bier drinken. Zij {2} niet roken hier.
steps.hint: He WANTS to drink beer. She MAY NOT smoke.

steps.q: 'Je moet op tijd komen' reflects the Dutch value of:

steps.s: Ik wil graag een {1} koffie en twee {2}.
steps.hint: A cup of coffee and two bread rolls
